title: Kefta tagine
subtitle: Moroccan spiced meatballs simmered in tomato sauce with eggs poached on top.
excerpt: Kefta tagine is a Moroccan one-pan dish of small spiced meatballs simmered in a thick tomato sauce, finished by breaking eggs directly into the sauce and covering until the whites set. The meatballs are seasoned with cumin, coriander, paprika, and a trace of cinnamon. Thirty-five minutes from start to table.
sourceNotes: A standard preparation across Moroccan home cooking and street food, documented consistently in twentieth-century Moroccan culinary literature. The kefta (spiced minced meat) braised in tomato sauce with eggs is the most common tagine preparation after lamb with preserved lemon. Beef is most common in urban Moroccan cooking; lamb is more typical in the countryside.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the meatballs and sauce without the eggs for up to three months. Defrost overnight in the fridge, reheat gently, and add the eggs fresh.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the meatballs and refrigerate for up to 24 hours before cooking.

recipeTools.notes: Wide, heavy-based pan with a lid. The meatballs and eggs both cook in the same pan.

content.text: Kefta tagine is the meatball dish of Moroccan cooking, and one of the most practical: the meatballs are small and cook through quickly in the sauce, and the eggs finish the dish in the final minutes. Everything happens in one pan. The spice mix (cumin, coriander, paprika, cinnamon) is straightforward but the proportion of fresh herbs in both the meatballs and the sauce keeps the dish from feeling flat.

Beef mince gives a slightly leaner result; a mix of beef and lamb gives more depth. The meatballs are small (roughly the size of a large marble) so they cook through in the sauce in eight to ten minutes without any preliminary browning, though browning first produces a better crust. Either approach works, and the no-browning version is faster on a weeknight.

The eggs go in at the end, broken directly into the sauce. Cover the pan and cook until the whites are just set and the yolks still soft. Each person breaks their own yolk into the sauce at the table. Bread is the right accompaniment for scooping.

Combine {{beef-mince}} of beef mince with the grated onion, chopped parsley, cumin, coriander, paprika, and cinnamon in a large bowl. Season generously with salt and black pepper. Mix with your hands for two minutes until the spices are evenly distributed. Roll into small balls, roughly 3 cm across. You should have about twenty.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the chopped onion half and cook for five minutes until softened. Add the garlic and cook for one minute. Stir in {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée, then add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, the extra cumin and paprika, and 100 ml of water. Season with salt and bring to a simmer.
Nestle the kefta into the sauce in a single layer. Cover and cook over medium-low heat for twelve minutes, turning the meatballs once halfway through. The sauce should be gently bubbling throughout.

Make four small wells in the sauce between the meatballs. Break one {{eggs}} egg into each well. Cover the pan and cook for four to five minutes until the whites are just set and the yolks remain soft. Scatter more chopped parsley over the top and bring the pan to the table.

items.symptom: The meatballs break apart in the sauce
items.cause: The mix was not worked long enough before shaping.
items.fix: Knead for a full two minutes before shaping. The mixture should feel cohesive and slightly sticky.

items.symptom: The eggs whites are still runny after five minutes
items.cause: The lid was not on properly, or the sauce temperature was too low.
items.fix: Check the lid is tight. Turn up the heat slightly. Add another minute of covered cooking.

items.cause: Too much water added, or lid not used.
items.fix: Remove the meatballs and reduce the sauce over high heat for three minutes, then return the meatballs and continue.

content.text: Kefta tagine is the most widely eaten tagine across Morocco — more common in daily cooking than the grander lamb preparations. The combination of spiced mince and eggs in tomato appears in various forms across North Africa and the Middle East, each with a different spice balance and degree of heat. The Moroccan version uses sweet spices in the meat (cinnamon, coriander) rather than chilli heat, which makes it approachable for most palates. In Morocco the dish is made in a clay tagine on a clay brazier; a heavy pan with a lid produces a comparable result.
